word_list.pronunciation: tˈeɪk ðə bɹˈɛd ˌaʊɾəv wˈʌnz mˈaʊθ

senses.definition: to deprive people of their most basic means of living, particularly via unfair mathods

word_list.pronunciation: ɹˈʌb sˈɑːlt ɪn ˌɪntʊ ðə wˈuːnd

senses.definition: to make a difficult or painful situation even worse for someone

examples.x: To hold them to a standard that discriminatory marriage bans made impossible to meet rubs salt in the wound of their loss.

senses.definition: to be tough and smart in negotiations, insisting on favorable terms to achieve a favorable outcome for oneself

examples.x: The company's prowess in driving a hard bargain has placed severe pressure on suppliers.
The car salesman knew he would have to drive a hard bargain with the customer who was a skilled negotiator.
She struck a hard bargain with the vendor, managing to get the price of the product reduced by 20%.
The businessman was renowned for driving a hard bargain, ensuring that every deal was profitable for his company.
After driving a hard bargain, she managed to secure a salary increase and better benefits at her new job.

senses.definition: an attitude or approach that is cruel, and often unrestricted

examples.x: They promised that the army would strike with an iron fist at any resistance.
The dictator ruled the country with an iron fist, suppressing dissent and opposition.
The principal managed the school with an iron fist, enforcing strict rules and discipline.
The CEO ran the company with an iron fist, making all the major decisions without input from employees.
The coach led the team with an iron fist, demanding rigorous training and unwavering discipline.

senses.definition: to cause a person experience great difficulty

examples.x: I believe in putting them through hell and making them earn it first.
When she leaves him in despair, he puts her through hell until she wins custody of their sons.
The abusive relationship put her through hell for years, causing both physical and emotional trauma.

word_list.pronunciation: twˈɪst tˈɜːn ðə nˈaɪf ɪnðə wˈuːnd

senses.definition: to purposefully make someone suffer more than they already do

examples.x: After the divorce, her friend turned the knife in the wound by saying she had always thought that the marriage wouldn't last.
Any more cuts in government aid to these poor people will be twisting the knife in the wound.
He said it was over, and then he turned the knife and said he had never loved me.
After losing the game, his opponent didn't just win but also decided to twist the knife by mocking his skills.
She had already received disappointing news, and her colleague's negative comments only served to turn the knife.

senses.definition: the use of excessive force, control, or authority in a way that can be harsh or oppressive

examples.x: The teacher ruled the classroom with a heavy hand, rarely allowing any freedom or creativity.
The new manager ruled with a heavy hand, imposing strict rules and micro-managing the employees, which led to low morale in the workplace.
In some countries, the government's heavy hand in controlling the media restricts freedom of speech and information.
The coach's heavy hand in training practices resulted in player burnout and injuries.

word_list.pronunciation: ˈaɪɚn hˈænd fˈɪst ɪn ɐ vˈɛlvɪt ɡlˈʌv

senses.definition: a person who appears gentle and kind on the outside, but they are firm, resolute, and even ruthless in their actions

examples.x: The patriarch has always been an iron fist in a velvet glove.
The new CEO has a reputation for being an iron hand in a velvet glove; she's charming and approachable, but she's also made some tough decisions to turn the company around.
He appears to be a lenient parent, but in reality, he's an iron hand in a velvet glove, setting clear boundaries and expectations for his children.
The diplomat used an iron hand in a velvet glove approach during the negotiations, appearing conciliatory while firmly protecting their country's interests.

senses.definition: to stop someone from acting freely, creatively, comfortably, or in their usual way

examples.x: Sarah's parents insisted on coming to the party, which really cramped her style because she couldn't relax and have fun with her friends.
Having to follow a strict dress code at work cramps my style; I prefer to wear casual and creative outfits.
Moving back home after college really cramped his style, as he no longer had the freedom to come and go as he pleased.
The new team rules are cramping his style, making it difficult for him to play the game the way he prefers.
Her partner's jealousy began to cramp her style, limiting her interactions with friends and colleagues.
The strict curfew really cramps my style; I can't stay out late with my friends anymore.
My neighbor's constant complaints about noise are starting to cramp my style; I can't have friends over as often as I'd like.
Sharing an office with someone who dislikes noise cramps my style because I enjoy listening to music while I work.
